title: Classifying Open-Ended Survey Responses
text: cat.survey thin domain wrapper around cat.stack injects survey-question context every prompt. call classify(input_data = ..., survey_question = "move?"), LLM sees: “respondent asked: move? answer : …” framing measurably improves accuracy open-ended survey data versus generic text classification, model uses question disambiguate short context-dependent responses. Everything else — supported models, output format, ensemble voting, batch mode — identical cat.stack.

title: Classifying Open-Ended Survey Responses
Classifying Open-Ended Survey Responses
code: responses <- c(   "Took a new job in Chicago",   "Wanted to be closer to grandkids",   "Couldn't afford rent in the Bay Area",   "Job market collapsed after the layoffs",   "Family pressure to move home" )  # Verbose category descriptions classify better than short labels. verbose_cats <- c(   "Job/school: A change in employment, education, or career, including transfers and retirement.",   "Family: Relationship changes, having children, supporting relatives, or relocating to be near family.",   "Cost of living: Housing affordability, cost of goods, or general economic pressure.",   "Other: The response does not fit any of the above categories." )  results <- classify(   input_data      = responses,   categories      = verbose_cats,   survey_question = "Why did you move to a new city?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"),   user_model      = "gpt-4o-mini" )

title: Classifying Open-Ended Survey Responses
text: Many survey responses fit one category (“moved new job closer family”). default classifier multi-label — results one 0/1 column per category, row can multiple 1s. force single-label, set add_other = FALSE shrink category list. make multi-label explicit analysis, use binary columns directly:
code: # Example downstream summary: library(dplyr) results %>%   dplyr::summarize(     pct_job    = mean(`Job/school`),     pct_family = mean(Family),     pct_cost   = mean(`Cost of living`),     pct_other  = mean(Other)   )

what: Discovering a category scheme when you don’t have one
title: Classifying Open-Ended Survey Responses
text: don’t already coding scheme, use extract() discover one responses , pass result classify(): See also extract.Rmd r-package/examples/ directory deeper walkthrough category discovery.
code: cats <- extract(   input_data      = responses,   survey_question = "Why did you move to a new city?",   max_categories  = 8L,   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY") ) cats$top_categories  # Optionally rewrite the labels to be more verbose, then classify: results <- classify(   input_data      = responses,   categories      = cats$top_categories,   survey_question = "Why did you move to a new city?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY") )

title: Classifying Open-Ended Survey Responses
text: Always set survey_question — ’s whole point using cat.survey cat.stack. Without might well use cat.stack::classify() directly. Write verbose category descriptions. label like "Family: relocating near family, child, divorce..." classifies several percentage points accurately just "Family". single biggest accuracy lever. Include “” category. Prevents model forcing ambiguous responses ill-fitting boxes. cat.survey prompt add one forget (add_other = "prompt" default). Validate hand-coded subsample. published research, never trust classifications without spot-checking human coding least 50–100 responses.

title: Classifying Open-Ended Survey Responses
text: Full Getting Started guide: vignette("getting-started", package = "cat.llm") Per-function reference: ?cat.survey::classify, ?cat.survey::extract, ?cat.survey::explore Empirical best-practices research (incl. verbose labels help) project Python README.

what: cat.survey — Open-ended survey response classification for R
title: cat.survey — Open-ended survey response classification for R
text: Survey response classification LLMs. domain wrapper around cat.stack adds survey_question context open-ended survey response analysis. cat.survey wraps Python cat-survey package via reticulate.

title: cat.survey — Open-ended survey response classification for R
code: # From R-universe (recommended) install.packages("cat.survey",                  repos = c("https://chrissoria.r-universe.dev",                           "https://cloud.r-project.org"))  # Or from a local clone devtools::install("path/to/cat.stack") devtools::install("path/to/cat.survey")  # Install the Python backend (one-time setup) # pip install cat-survey

title: cat.survey — Open-ended survey response classification for R
code: library(cat.survey)  results <- classify(   input_data      = df$responses,   categories      = c("Economic", "Family", "Education", "Other"),   survey_question = "Why did you move to this city?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY") )

title: cat.survey — Open-ended survey response classification for R
code: result <- extract(   input_data      = df$responses,   survey_question = "What do you like about your neighborhood?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY") ) print(result$top_categories)

title: cat.survey — Open-ended survey response classification for R
code: raw_cats <- explore(   input_data      = df$responses,   survey_question = "Why did you move?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"),   iterations      = 3L )

title: cat.survey — Open-ended survey response classification for R

what: Classify survey responses using LLMs — classify
title: Classify survey responses using LLMs — classify
text: Wraps Python cat_survey.classify() function. Adds survey_question context base cat.stack classification engine.

title: Classify survey responses using LLMs — classify
code: classify(   input_data,   categories,   survey_question = "",   description = "",   add_other = "prompt",   check_verbosity = TRUE,   api_key = NULL,   user_model = "gpt-4o",   mode = "image",   creativity = NULL,   safety = FALSE,   chain_of_verification = FALSE,   chain_of_thought = FALSE,   step_back_prompt = FALSE,   context_prompt = FALSE,   thinking_budget = 0L,   example1 = NULL,   example2 = NULL,   example3 = NULL,   example4 = NULL,   example5 = NULL,   example6 = NULL,   filename = NULL,   save_directory = NULL,   model_source = "auto",   max_categories = 12L,   categories_per_chunk = 10L,   divisions = 10L,   research_question = NULL,   models = NULL,   consensus_threshold = "unanimous",   use_json_schema = TRUE,   max_workers = NULL,   fail_strategy = "partial",   max_retries = 5L,   batch_retries = 2L,   retry_delay = 1,   row_delay = 0,   pdf_dpi = 150L,   auto_download = FALSE )

title: Classify survey responses using LLMs — classify
text: input_data character vector, list, data.frame column survey responses classify. categories character vector category names, "auto" infer categories data. survey_question Character. survey question text. Default "". description Character. Additional context classification task. Default "". add_other Logical "prompt". Controls addition "" category. Default "prompt". check_verbosity Logical. Check category descriptions. Default TRUE. api_key API key model provider (single-model mode). user_model Character. Model name. Default "gpt-4o". mode Character. PDF processing mode. Default "image". creativity Numeric NULL. Temperature. Default NULL. safety Logical. Save progress item. Default FALSE. chain_of_verification Logical. Default FALSE. chain_of_thought Logical. Default FALSE. step_back_prompt Logical. Default FALSE. context_prompt Logical. Default FALSE. thinking_budget Integer. Extended thinking budget. Default 0L. example1, example2, example3, example4, example5, example6 Optional -shot examples. filename Character NULL. Output CSV filename. save_directory Character NULL. Output directory. model_source Character. Provider hint. Default "auto". max_categories Integer. Max categories auto mode. Default 12L. categories_per_chunk Integer. Default 10L. divisions Integer. Default 10L. research_question Character NULL. Optional research context. models List model specs ensemble mode. consensus_threshold Character numeric. Default "unanimous". use_json_schema Logical. Default TRUE. max_workers Integer NULL. Default NULL. fail_strategy Character. Default "partial". max_retries Integer. Default 5L. batch_retries Integer. Default 2L. retry_delay Numeric. Default 1.0. row_delay Numeric. Default 0.0. pdf_dpi Integer. Default 150L. auto_download Logical. Default FALSE.

title: Classify survey responses using LLMs — classify
Classify survey responses using LLMs — classify
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ results <- classify(   input_data      = c("Took a new job in Chicago",                       "Wanted to be closer to grandkids",                       "Couldn't afford rent in the Bay Area"),   categories      = c("Job/school", "Family", "Cost of living", "Other"),   survey_question = "Why did you move?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"),   user_model      = "gpt-4o-mini" ) } # }

what: Explore raw categories in survey response data — explore
title: Explore raw categories in survey response data — explore
text: Wraps Python cat_survey.explore() function. Returns every category string extracted every chunk across every iteration – duplicates intact. Useful analysing category stability saturation.

title: Explore raw categories in survey response data — explore
code: explore(   input_data,   api_key,   survey_question = "",   description = "",   max_categories = 12L,   categories_per_chunk = 10L,   divisions = 12L,   user_model = "gpt-4o",   creativity = NULL,   specificity = "broad",   research_question = NULL,   filename = NULL,   model_source = "auto",   iterations = 8L,   random_state = NULL,   focus = NULL,   chunk_delay = 0 )

title: Explore raw categories in survey response data — explore
text: input_data character vector, list, data.frame column survey responses. api_key Character. API key model provider. survey_question Character. survey question text. Default "". description Character. Additional context. Default "". max_categories Integer. Max categories per chunk. Default 12L. categories_per_chunk Integer. Default 10L. divisions Integer. Number data chunks. Default 12L. user_model Character. Model name. Default "gpt-4o". creativity Numeric NULL. Temperature. Default NULL. specificity Character. "broad" "specific". Default "broad". research_question Character NULL. Optional research context. filename Character NULL. Output CSV filename. model_source Character. Provider hint. Default "auto". iterations Integer. Number passes. Default 8L. random_state Integer NULL. Random seed. focus Character NULL. Optional focus. chunk_delay Numeric. Seconds API calls. Default 0.0.

title: Explore raw categories in survey response data — explore
text: character vector every category string extracted.

title: Explore raw categories in survey response data — explore
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ raw_categories <- explore(   input_data      = df$open_response,   survey_question = "What concerns you most about your community?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"),   user_model      = "gpt-4o-mini",   iterations      = 4L ) table(raw_categories) } # }

what: Extract categories from survey responses using LLMs — extract
title: Extract categories from survey responses using LLMs — extract
text: Wraps Python cat_survey.extract() function. Discovers returns normalised, deduplicated set categories found survey response data.

title: Extract categories from survey responses using LLMs — extract
code: extract(   input_data,   api_key,   survey_question = "",   description = "",   input_type = "text",   max_categories = 12L,   categories_per_chunk = 10L,   divisions = 12L,   user_model = "gpt-4o",   creativity = NULL,   specificity = "broad",   research_question = NULL,   mode = "text",   filename = NULL,   model_source = "auto",   iterations = 8L,   random_state = NULL,   focus = NULL,   chunk_delay = 0 )

title: Extract categories from survey responses using LLMs — extract
text: input_data character vector, list, data.frame column survey responses. api_key Character. API key model provider. survey_question Character. survey question text. Default "". description Character. Additional context. Default "". input_type Character. Type input. Default "text". max_categories Integer. Maximum final categories. Default 12L. categories_per_chunk Integer. Default 10L. divisions Integer. Number data chunks. Default 12L. user_model Character. Model name. Default "gpt-4o". creativity Numeric NULL. Temperature. Default NULL. specificity Character. "broad" "specific". Default "broad". research_question Character NULL. Optional research context. mode Character. Processing mode. Default "text". filename Character NULL. Output CSV filename. model_source Character. Provider hint. Default "auto". iterations Integer. Number passes. Default 8L. random_state Integer NULL. Random seed. focus Character NULL. Optional focus. chunk_delay Numeric. Seconds API calls. Default 0.0.

title: Extract categories from survey responses using LLMs — extract
Extract categories from survey responses using LLMs — extract
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ result <- extract(   input_data      = c("Took a new job in Chicago",                       "Wanted to be closer to grandkids",                       "Couldn't afford rent in the Bay Area"),   survey_question = "Why did you move?",   api_key         = Sys.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"),   user_model      = "gpt-4o-mini" ) print(result$top_categories) } # }

title: cat.survey 0.1.0
text: Initial release R interface Python catsurvey package. Thin domain wrapper around cat.stack injects survey-specific prompt framing (“respondent asked: …”) classification, extraction, exploration calls. Exports classify(), extract(), explore().
